entry.title: Re: Blog 3 - From BW 7.5 to SAP BDC: Reusing Extractors, Custom Logic, and Models Standard Extracto
entry.content:
@MKreitlein Currently we still have enough ADSOs in our BW/4 which are based on extractors without key fields which are based on cube like content with additive / sum approach and not move. As far as I understand that this does not work anymore with Datasphere / BDC (which is one reason why Bridge makes sense). There is not only the BW/4 content but also a lot of content from before (this is not available in BW/4 anymore for fresh install) which is migrated and working. 

We tried to find keys for 2LIS_04_P_ARBPL_20 which was no fun. With this extractor another problem was that delta have to be reimplemented partially by us because changes not always are recognized. This was a very time consuming and no fun process. It seems to be working now but this is kind of reverse enigneering.

BR, Torsten

entry.title: Why Your Agent Won't Behave: Determinism, Drift, and the Architecture That Contains It. (Part 2)
entry.content:
Deterministic Systems vs. Non-Deterministic AI

What Determinism Actually Means

A deterministic system is one where the same input always produces the same output. No randomness, no ambiguity, no variation. The entire history of software engineering — compilers, databases, operating systems, network protocols — is built on this property.

Determinism gives you three things that engineers take for granted:

Reproducibility. You can replay a bug, bisect a regression, write a test that fails today and passes tomorrow only if the code changed.

Composability. You can chain predictable functions together and the result stays predictable. If each step behaves the same way every time, so does the whole pipeline.

Accountability. When something goes wrong, you can trace exactly why. The audit trail is complete.

These aren't luxuries. For most enterprise systems — billing, compliance, transaction processing, access control — determinism isn't optional. You need to know what happens next, and you need it to happen the same way every time.

Where Language Models Introduce Non-Determinism

A common misconception: setting temperature to zero should make an LLM deterministic. Mathematically, it looks like it should. Temperature zero means the model just picks the token with the highest probability at each step — no randomness, same input, same output.

In practice, you get different outputs from identical prompts. Why? Because the non-determinism doesn't come from the model's logic. It comes from the hardware.

GPU floating-point arithmetic doesn't behave like textbook math. When billions of calculations run in parallel across thousands of cores, tiny rounding errors accumulate differently depending on the execution order. Change which GPU you run on, which batch you're grouped with, or even which driver version is installed, and you get subtly different numbers percolating through the computation. One bit flip in the 15th decimal place can flip which token the model chooses.

Real-world benchmarks confirm this. When researchers tested major models like Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini at temperature zero across repeated identical runs, short structured prompts stayed highly consistent — but open-ended reasoning prompts that generate hundreds of tokens diverged sharply, with some models producing a different output on nearly every run. Same prompt, different results.

The takeaway: this isn't a bug. It's a fundamental property of running probability calculations on distributed hardware. You can't eliminate it without trading away performance or using slower, more expensive exact-arithmetic systems. It's an engineering tradeoff, not a flaw in the model.

How Chain-of-Thought Amplifies Variability

Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning works by breaking a complex problem into steps. Instead of jumping straight to the answer, the model generates intermediate reasoning: "First, let me consider the cost... then the timeline... therefore the answer is X."

The catch: each step is probabilistic. The model picks reasoning tokens from a probability distribution, and each token becomes context for the next choice. This creates a branching structure. Pick "cost first" as your opening thought, and you've reshaped the entire downstream reasoning. Pick "timeline first," and you're on a completely different path to (maybe) a different answer.

Recent research adds a further twist: the visible reasoning text might not be where the actual reasoning is happening. Internal analysis shows that LLMs often encode answers in their hidden layers before they write out the reasoning. The surface-level chain of thought might be more of a scaffold that structures the generation than a faithful record of how the model actually thinks.

The practical implication: CoT reasoning is valuable for improving accuracy, but it's not a deterministic scaffold. It's itself a source of variability. The longer and more complex the reasoning chain, the more opportunities for small divergences to compound.

Compounding Variance, Not Chaos

It's tempting to call this the "butterfly effect" — and some researchers do. But there's an important difference worth understanding.

Chaotic systems (weather, turbulence) are deterministic — they follow fixed laws — but tiny input changes lead to wildly different outcomes. Agentic AI is different. It's probabilistic at every step. Small changes in early reasoning steps compound into bigger divergences downstream.

The good news: formal analysis shows this compounding is bounded, not explosive. Error doesn't grow exponentially. It grows more slowly — roughly like the square root of the number of steps. That means the system degrades predictably. You can engineer around it.

How to Evaluate Non-Deterministic Systems

Testing a deterministic function is straightforward: assert that this input always produces that output. Non-deterministic systems need a different approach.

Run evaluations multiple times. One run tells you almost nothing. Run your eval 5-10 times and look at the distribution. Does it consistently hit 90%? Or does it swing between 70% and 100%? Both average to similar numbers, but they're completely different systems.

Define success as a range, not a point. Instead of "the output must be X," say "the output must be one of these acceptable responses" or "must score above this semantic similarity threshold." For code, run the tests. For math, check if the answer is correct. Let deterministic validators do the heavy lifting.

Watch the variance. If your agent is wildly inconsistent, it's operating at a decision boundary — a place where tiny variations flip the outcome. That's a design problem worth fixing.

The Practitioner's Mental Model

Think of an agentic AI system as three concentric rings.

The outer ring is deterministic infrastructure: API gateways, authentication, rate limiting, database transactions, audit logging, workflow state machines. This is traditional software engineering and it must stay that way. These components need to know — with certainty — what happens next.

The middle ring is the control plane: orchestration logic, retry policies, timeout handling, output validation, schema enforcement, human-in-the-loop gates. This is where you manage stochastic behavior without eliminating it. As SAP has demonstrated with its Autonomous Enterprise architecture, governance composes as you add capabilities — when you combine two controlled agents, the governance constraints compose together, not dilute. The middle ring is deterministic in its structure (the rules are fixed) but adaptive in its responses (it reacts to variable AI outputs).

The inner ring is the AI model itself: reasoning, planning, generating, deciding. This is where non-determinism lives, and where it should be allowed to live. The model's value comes precisely from its ability to handle ambiguity, generalize from sparse examples, and produce contextually appropriate outputs that no rule system could enumerate in advance.

The mistake is letting the inner ring's properties leak into the outer ring. The skill is building a middle ring robust enough to prevent that.

Closing: An Enterprise Architecture Perspective

For enterprise architects evaluating agentic AI, the question isn't "deterministic or non-deterministic?" It's "where does each belong in my architecture?"

The ten patterns from Part 1 aren't academic abstractions — they map directly to familiar enterprise concerns. Agentic RAG maps to your existing content and knowledge management layer. HITL maps to your approval and governance workflows. State machine orchestration maps to your BPM and process automation layer. Tool-use routing maps to your API gateway and service mesh. The AI model itself is a new type of compute resource — powerful but probabilistic — that plugs into these existing layers rather than replacing them.

The enterprise systems that work will be the ones that treat determinism and non-determinism as complementary materials, not competing philosophies. Your transaction processing, your compliance rules, your audit trails — these remain deterministic, as they must. Your natural language understanding, your document reasoning, your adaptive decision-making — these benefit from the flexibility that probabilistic models provide.

But as SAP's leadership has emphasized in its Autonomous Enterprise messaging, simply plugging AI agents into your existing system landscape drives little value on its own. Moving to an autonomous enterprise requires serious change management — AI adoption goes hand in hand with business process change and end-user enablement.

The architecture that emerges looks less like "AI replaces the system" and more like "AI augments specific nodes in a well-governed system." The workflow knows what happens next. The AI decides how it happens at the steps where rigid rules fall short. Humans provide strategic direction. Agents execute with guardrails.

That's not a compromise. That's good engineering.

Missed Part 1? "Tame Your Agents: 10 Design Patterns for Agentic AI That Actually Work in Production" covers the practical playbook. Next up, Part 3 maps everything onto SAP's AI landscape — Joule, Joule Studio, and the Skill-vs-Agent split that makes this whole determinism discussion concrete.

References

Non-Determinism in LLMs

Floating-Point Non-Associativity

  • Goldberg, D. (1991). What Every Computer Scientist Should Know About Floating-Point Arithmetic. ACM Computing Surveys, 23(1), 5–48. doi:10.1145/103162.103163
  • Šubonis, M. (2025). Zero Temperature Randomness in LLMs. martynassubonis.substack.com

Batch Variance and Infrastructure Heterogeneity

  • SugiV. (2025). Unmasking the True Culprit: Why Temperature=0 Doesn't Mean Deterministic LLM Inference. blog.sugiv.fyi (Empirical analysis of batch-size-dependent non-determinism via GPU kernel reduction order.)
  • Brenndoerfer, M. (2025). Why Temperature=0 Doesn't Guarantee Determinism in LLMs. mbrenndoerfer.com (Server heterogeneity, GPU driver variation, and MoE routing effects.)
  • QAnswer. (2026). Why LLMs Are Not Deterministic Even at Temperature 0. qanswer.ai (Benchmarks across Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini showing determinism rates at temperature zero.)
  • Atil, B., et al. (2025). Non-Determinism of "Deterministic" LLM Settings. arXiv:2408.04667 (Systematic study of output variance across 5 models, 8 tasks, and 10 runs per configuration.)

Compounding Variance in Agentic Systems

  • Xu, Y., et al. (2026). Agentic Confidence Calibration. arXiv:2601.15778 (Holistic Trajectory Calibration for compounding errors across agent trajectories.)
  • Guo, A., et al. (2026). Information Fidelity in Tool-Using LLM Agents: A Martingale Analysis of the Model Context Protocol. arXiv:2602.13320 (Formal proof that error propagation grows sublinearly — O(√T), not exponentially.)
  • Singh, A., et al. (2025). Stochastic, Dynamic, Fluid Autonomy in Agentic AI: Implications for Authorship, Inventorship, and Liability. arXiv:2504.04058 (Analysis of compounded stochasticity across recursive agentic workflows.)

Statistical Evaluation of Agentic Systems

  • Rein, D., et al. (2024). Stochasticity in Agentic Evaluations: Quantifying Inconsistency with Intraclass Correlation. arXiv:2512.06710 (ICC-based framework for measuring trial-to-trial variance in agentic benchmarks.)

Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

  • Wei, J., Wang, X., Schuurmans, D., Bosma, M., Xia, F., Chi, E. H., Le, Q. V., & Zhou, D. (2022). Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models. NeurIPS 2022. arXiv:2201.11903
  • Anonymous. (2026). LLM Reasoning Is Latent, Not the Chain of Thought. arXiv:2604.15726 (Position paper arguing reasoning should be studied as latent-state trajectory formation rather than faithful surface CoT.)

Survey and Overview Papers

  • Wang, L., Ma, C., Feng, X., et al. (2023). A Survey on Large Language Model Based Autonomous Agents. arXiv:2308.11432
  • Weng, L. (2023). LLM-Powered Autonomous Agents. Lil'Log. lilianweng.github.io
  • Xi, Z., et al. (2023). The Rise and Potential of Large Language Model Based Agents: A Survey. arXiv:2309.07864

 

This is Part 2 of a four-part series. Part 1 — "Tame Your Agents" is the builder's playbook: ten design patterns, when to use each, and the guardrails that keep agents in line. Part 2 (this post) goes under the hood: why LLMs are non-deterministic by nature, how that variability compounds across steps, how to evaluate systems that won't repeat themselves, and the three-ring architecture that puts it all together. Part 3 — "The SAP AI Landscape" maps these patterns onto SAP's agentic stack (Joule, Joule Studio, Skills vs. Agents, AI Core, MCP/A2A). Part 4 — "Hands-On" builds a simple Joule agent end to end.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 17 - Schema evolution testing
Re: Suitable Practice System for GUI based ABAP development
entry.content:
While there is no future, as already noted, for SAP GUI development there is however an awful lot of present development which will not be thrown away any time soon and which will need to be maintained, so the desire to learn is positive.

At the moment the ABAP Cloud Developer Trial on Docker is not available and this would have been my first recommendation because it is easy to get started. Read the whole thread first, so you'll be able to decided whether to wait or not.

If you want to get started now, an older option is to set up your own Linux VM and install AS ABAP 7.52 SP04, Developer Edition: this requires substantially more effort and knowledge, the process is fairly well documented but familiarity with Linux and cmdline tools is between "helpful" and "required". Word of caution here, too: it has been EOL'd, so get it before the deadline or it is gone forever.

entry.title: Re: UK Plastic Tax - Questions on Stock Transfers
entry.content:
Q1: RDP uses only GI_ST material documents for the re-allocation of refundable inventory from one plant to another plant. The aggregated quantities of GR_ST material documents are shown in Analyze Report Data, but I'd say it's more for cross-checking purposes (in the past they were used in the calculation logic, but not anymore).

Q2: The two-step stock transfer is also supported. Maybe it's just not listed in the documentation

Q3: I believe you're right that we should include the "in transit" stock. Check if this "in transit" stock is considered as increase on the receiving plant. For example if Plant A issues (with mvt. type 641) 100 pieces and they are in transit to Plant B, my expectation is that the stock on Plant A is already reduced, while the inventory of Plant B will include the 100 pieces, if the "in transit" stock is also selected. If the in transit stock is not selected, then these 100 pieces are temporarily "lost".

entry.title: Re: Default CGI node ScvLvl NURG
entry.content:
Hi,
I assume you are referring to our CGI_CT_V9 DMEEX trees. You can redefine our logic in your copy of the DMEEX tree where you can make a constant if you really want to always output only NURG in case of non-SEPA. Alternatively you can maintain default instruction key on the house bank. Still you need to adapt a condition in the DMEEX tree so that this default is output only in case of non-SEPA. Always depends on the specific use case. Standard trees are only templates.
Kind regards,
Ondrej

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 17 - Schema evolution testing
SII Spain – Conflict between Operation Date/Fiscal Year Period and invoice date in SD sales orders:
entry.content:
Dear community;

We are working with S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and the SII (Suministro Inmediato de Información) integration for the Spanish Tax Agency (AEAT). We have encountered a recurring issue affecting several types of SD sales orders, and we would like to know whether a standard solution exists or if this is a known gap.

System behavior:
For customer invoices (F2) generated from SD sales orders (services), the system determines the Operation Date and Fiscal Year/Period fields sent to the SII by reading the "Requested Service Date" from the sales order. If this field is empty, the system falls back to the invoice date on the sales order. The Invoice Issue Date, on the other hand, always takes the actual invoice issue date.

The problem:
We have three operational scenarios where the requested service date on the sales order differs from the invoice issue date, causing the Operation Date and Fiscal Year/Period to diverge from the invoice date and triggering conflicts when submitting to the SII:

  1. Services billed in advance: rental contracts where invoicing occurs before the service period begins. The service date falls in the month after the invoice is issued.
  2. Rebilling of prior periods: penalties or demurrage charges billed in the current month but relating to services rendered in previous months.
  3. Material sales orders with an adjusted value date: the value date is modified to control payment due dates correctly, and the system uses it to determine the Fiscal Year/Period sent to the SII.

In all three cases, the current workaround is manually editing the Tax Fulfillment Date on the posted invoice, which the accounting team needs to eliminate.

What we have already checked:
We have not found any SSCUI or standard configuration option in Public Cloud Edition that allows controlling the source of the Operation Date / Fiscal Year/Period fields on SD invoices, beyond leaving the requested service date blank on the sales order — which is not viable for all scenarios.

The question:
Has anyone resolved any of these scenarios in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition without relying on manual post-posting corrections? Is there standard configuration we may have missed, or is this a gap?

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Re: VIES Validation - Business Partner
entry.content:
Hi Marcel,

Yes, there is a standard option for this. VIES validation can be triggered when Business Partner master data is saved, provided that the relevant validation spot is activated.

For VIES validation, the supported trigger points are:

  1. Business Partner master data save
  2. Manual/periodic validation using the app Online Validation for Multiple Partners
  3. Sales order creation
  4. Billing document creation

So, for your requirement to validate the VAT number when a new Business Partner is created, a custom extension is not required just to trigger the VIES validation. The relevant Business Partner validation spot needs to be activated/configured.

Best,

Anne

entry.title: Join Our Live Session: Quality Planning in SAP S/4HANA Quality Management
entry.content:
Date: September 29, 2026
Time: 10:00 AM – 11:30 AM

Quality Planning is a fundamental component of SAP S/4HANA Quality Management, providing the framework that determines what is inspected, how inspections are carried out, and when they are triggered within business processes.

In this live session, we will explore the key elements of Quality Planning and their role in controlling and standardizing quality inspection processes. Participants will gain an understanding of how inspection types, master inspection characteristics, and inspection plans work together to ensure consistent quality management across procurement and production processes.

The session will also demonstrate how these planning elements drive the automatic creation of inspection lots during logistical events such as goods receipts and production confirmations, enabling efficient and controlled quality execution.

Whether you are new to SAP Quality Management or looking to strengthen your understanding of quality planning concepts, this session will provide valuable insights into the master data structures that support effective quality processes in SAP S/4HANA.

What You Will Learn

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

  • Explain the role of Quality Planning within SAP S/4HANA Quality Management.
  • Describe how inspection types in the material master determine when quality inspections are triggered.
  • Identify the purpose of master inspection characteristics in defining inspection requirements.
  • Explain how inspection plans structure and standardize quality inspection processes.
  • Understand how quality planning elements enable the automatic creation of inspection lots during logistical events.

entry.title: Business Partner Migration from R3 into S4 Hana public cloud
entry.content:
Hi all,

we are designing the customer and supplier master concept for an S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition migration and would value experience from anyone who has solved this.

Scenario: we need to harmonize legacy R/3 customers (debtors) and suppliers (creditors) into a single Business Partner, but keep the original customer and supplier numbers as the operative KUNNR and LIFNR, because several 3rd-party systems use these numbers as primary keys. The legacy debtor and creditor ranges overlap (debtor 123 and creditor 123 can be different entities), so same-number for the legacy population is not possible.

Our preferred target, but we have never implemented it:
- The BP gets its own neutral internal number.
- KUNNR and LIFNR keep their different legacy numbers via external assignment (Flexible Grouping, "Same Numbers" off in SSCUI 102741 and 102742).
- A dual-role entity is one BP with, for example, BP 50001, KUNNR 123, LIFNR 456 (three different numbers).
- New business partners going forward use same-number (BP = KUNNR = LIFNR).

What we tried so far: we deactivated "Same Numbers" and used the two-step approach (migrate Customer, then "Supplier - extend existing record by new org levels"). In our tests the BP, customer and supplier always end up with the identical number, so we keep getting same-number behavior and cannot produce three different numbers on one BP. References we worked from: SAP Note 2748819 and KBA 3393756.

We also evaluated the alternatives, but each has a significant downside, which is why we prefer the approach above:
- Storing the legacy numbers as BP identification numbers/types (the SAP-standard route): would force custom resolution logic in every single 3rd-party interface, an anti-pattern for our landscape.
- Separate Business Partners for the debtor and the creditor role: goes against the one-BP-per-entity model, and we hit known issues with duplicate identification numbers in another project.
- Customer-side data cleansing and renumbering: would mean changing the primary keys in the downstream systems, which is exactly what we must avoid.

Questions:
1) Is our preferred target (one BP, different KUNNR and LIFNR, neutral BP number) actually achievable in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and would you recommend it?
2) What is the correct configuration and migration sequence to end up with three different numbers on one BP? What are we most likely missing, given our attempts keep collapsing to same-number?
3) Any pitfalls at scale (100k+ records), e.g. CVIV_UI 6 "Enter External Number" or CVI_EI011/CVI_EI041?
4) For a dual-role entity, is Customer first plus "Supplier - extend existing record by new org levels" the right path, and how do you keep the two numbers distinct without a need for special logic in the underlying interfaces?

Thanks a lot for any working configuration, steps, or lessons learned.

entry.title: Re: Mass update Settlement Dates in Condition Contract
entry.content:
@priya_sshekhar  

You can propose a solution in either of the following two ways:

1)Ask users to update the settlement calendars monthly for both the Delta Accruals and Final Settlement sections in the Condition Contract manually when creating the contract. By doing so, the settlement calendars will be generated automatically each time the contract is saved.
2)Adjust the configuration under Node: Specify Settlement Settings for Condition Contract Types. Select the relevant contract type and maintain the settlement calendars in the configuration. This will enable the settlement calendars to be generated automatically for both Delta Accruals and Final Settlement. (This way you can Minimize the manual maintenance of adding calendars to contract manually)

entry.title: Re: the impact when switch a RFC from non-unicode to unicode
entry.content:
In this case, as both sides (LUP and GRC) are Unicode systems, there will probably be less problems when switching the connection to Unicode than there were with Non-Unicode:

a) Unicode supports more characters than any of the Non-Unicode languages, so there will be less invalid characters and therefore less #-signs in the received data.

b) The performance will be significantly better, because we are saving two codepage conversions! Consider what happens during RFC communication, when two Unicode systems communicate: both systems already have all data in Unicode format.

When making an RFC call with a Non-Unicode destination:

  • sending system takes the data in Unicode format (e.g. from a DB table etc.)
  • then converts it to the NUC codepage
  • then sends it over the network connection
  • the receiving system then converts it from NUC codepage back to Unicode
  • then processes it/stores it in DB tables etc.

When the destination is switched to Unicode, this process looks like this:

  • sending system takes the data from DB table
  • sends it over the network connection as is
  • the receiving system receives it already in Unicode format
  • and then processes/stores it in DB tables as is.

Neither the sending nor the receiving system needs to perform a codepage conversion, and codepage conversions are expensive operations!

entry.title: Re: SAP .NET Connector not working anymore
entry.content:
Why do you think, this is a SAP .NET Connector problem?

  • The error message comes from the operating system, not from NCo.
  • And apparently the error happens inside an ODBC driver, not inside NCo!

Of course, as Markus already pointed out, the exhaustive memory consumption might happen anywhere, even in NCo, but there is no proof yet! Basically, there are multiple possibilities:

  • the exhaustive memory consumption might happen inside another process, not even in the Power BI process. The Power BI process just runs into this error, because that other process has already consumed all the memory that the operating system has to offer...
  • The out-of-memory situation happens, because the Power BI is trying to process a data set that is too big for the machine. In that case, it can be fixed by installing more RAM on the machine, or by dividing the one big data set into several smaller ones.
  • The Power BI program has a memory leak. But such a leak can be anywhere, in the ODBC driver used here, in NCo, in Power BI coding, etc.

In short: this problem first needs to be investigated by a Windows expert on OS level.

entry.title: Re: Transport Request tab/Page is not showing
entry.content:
Hi,

Regarding your issue, you need to go to your YYMF transaction change document->click button 'configure page' on top right, if you don't see this button, click 'Personalize' at the top of the page->in it you could enable the configuration mode by check the checkbox. When you click 'configure page', in section 'View'->you will see available assignment block 'Transport management'->move it to display column->save&close the popup window. Refresh your YYMF page, you will see the Transport management AB, there you could create TRs then.

BR

entry.title: Re: SAP .NET Connector not working anymore
entry.content: I don't think, a SAP ticket should be opened for this. As I wrote in my comment above, the problem could be anywhere, in another process running simultaneously to Power BI, in the ODBC driver, in Power BI itself or in NCo. First a Windows expert should try to narrow this down, and only if the evidence really points to a memory leak in NCo, a SAP ticket should be opened.
